params.text:
Assessing and controlling risk

Under the Health and Safety at Work Act and related regulations, employers must assess risks and put sensible controls in place. A slip and trip risk assessment looks at the floors, the tasks, the environment and the people, then decides what needs to change.

A useful way to think about it is: spot the hazard, decide who might be harmed, control the risk, and review it regularly. You play a vital part by reporting new hazards as they appear – a risk assessment is only as good as the everyday awareness that keeps it current.

params.question: You notice a corridor light has blown, leaving a set of steps in shadow. What is the best action?

answers.text: Report it promptly so the light can be replaced, and take care until it is fixed

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Reporting the defect gets it repaired and helps keep the risk assessment up to date.

answers.text: Ignore it – someone else will probably notice

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Poor lighting is a real hazard. Assuming someone else will act means it may be left, and a fall could result.

answers.text: Wait until several lights have failed before reporting them together

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Delaying leaves a known hazard in place. A single failed light over steps should be reported straight away.

answers.text: Tell colleagues verbally but do not record it anywhere

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A quick word helps, but the fault still needs to be reported properly so it is actually repaired.
